MS-11
The recommendations for 5-FU/leucovorin and gemcitabine are based on 
results of the ESPAC-3 trial.27 In this phase III, randomized, open-label 
trial, patients with ampullary, bile duct, or other periampullary cancers 
were randomized to 5-FU/leucovorin (n = 100), gemcitabine (n = 92), or 
observation (n = 105). The median survival for each arm was 57.8, 70.8, 
and 40.6 months, respectively. Statistical comparisons between treatment 
arms were not reported for individual cancer types. When data for all 3 
cancer types were combined, there was no significant difference in 
survival between treatment arms, but the HR for chemotherapy compared 
to observation was significant (P = .03). The rate of treatment-related 
serious AEs was higher in those receiving 5-FU/leucovorin than in those 
receiving gemcitabine (49% vs. 30%; P = .002). Based on the high level of 
evidence presented in this study, the Panel assigned a category 1 
designation to 5-FU/leucovorin and gemcitabine for the adjuvant treatment 
of resected ampullary adenocarcinoma. The recommendation for 
gemcitabine is also based on data from CONKO-001 investigating 
adjuvant therapy in pancreatic cancer.127
The recommendation for capecitabine/gemcitabine is based on 
extrapolation of data from ESPAC-4.128 ESPAC-4, a phase III, 
randomized, open-label trial, tested gemcitabine monotherapy or 
capecitabine/gemcitabine as adjuvant treatment for resected pancreatic 
cancer. A total of 730 patients were included in the final analysis, 366 in 
the gemcitabine arm and 364 in the capecitabine/gemcitabine arm. The 
median OS was 25.5 months and 28 months in the gemcitabine and 
gemcitabine + capecitabine arm, respectively (P = .032). While 54% of 
patients in the monotherapy group experienced any grade 3–4 AEs, this 
rate was 63% in the combination arm.
The recommendation for cisplatin/gemcitabine is extrapolated from a 
phase II randomized trial that enrolled 410 patients with advanced biliary 
tract cancer.129 Ampullary cancers were included, but the exact number
was not reported. Patients with locally advanced or metastatic disease 
were randomly assigned to receive either cisplatin/gemcitabine (n = 204) 
or gemcitabine monotherapy (n = 206). The median OS and median 
progression-free survival (PFS) were significantly longer in the 
combination group versus the monotherapy group: OS, 11.7 vs. 8.1 
months; P < .001 and PFS, 8.0 vs. 5.0 months; P < .001, respectively. The 
rates of any grade 3–4 AEs were similar between the 2 arms (70.7% vs. 
68.8% for combination and single agent, respectively). 
Cisplatin/gemcitabine was not tested in the adjuvant setting in this trial, but 
other smaller trials tested adjuvant cisplatin/gemcitabine after surgery for 
biliary cancers.130,131
The recommendation for CapeOx is extrapolated from a phase II trial that 
enrolled a total of 31 patients with advanced small bowel and ampullary 
cancers, 12 of whom had ampullary cancer.132 All patients had metastatic 
or unresectable tumors and had not received prior systemic 
chemotherapy. All patients in this trial received CapeOx. The overall 
response rate was 50%, and the median OS was 20.4 months. CapeOx 
was not tested in the adjuvant setting in this trial. The Panel deems 
FOLFOX a reasonable alternative to CapeOx based on clinical experience 
with these agents in periampullary cancers. In addition, the 
recommendation of FOLFOX or CapeOx adjuvant therapy for intestinal 
type ampullary cancers is also derived from trials testing these agents in 
colon cancer.133,134
The recommendation for capecitabine is based on extrapolation of data 
from BILCAP, a phase III randomized trial that enrolled 447 patients with 
biliary tract cancer (cholangiocarcinoma or muscle-invasive gallbladder 
cancer).135 In this study, patients were randomly assigned to capecitabine 
(n = 223) or observation (n = 224) after surgery. After a median follow-up 
of 60 months, the median OS was statistically similar between the two 
arms (51.1 vs. 36.4 months in the capecitabine vs. observation groups,

respectively; P = .097). Serious AEs were observed in 21% of patients in 
the capecitabine group and 10% of patients in the observation group. The 
recommendation for capecitabine adjuvant therapy for intestinal type 
ampullary adenocarcinoma is derived from the X-ACT study in colon 
cancer.136
The recommendation for mFOLFIRINOX is based on extrapolation of data 
from the phase III PRODIGE 24/CCTG PA.6 randomized trial in resected 
pancreatic cancer.137 Patients (n = 493) with pancreatic cancer were 
randomly assigned postoperatively to receive mFOLFIRINOX (n = 247) or 
gemcitabine (n = 246). The median PFS and median OS were significantly 
longer in the mFOLFIRINOX group compared to the gemcitabine group 
(21.6 vs. 12.8 months; P < .001; and 54.4 vs. 35.0 months; P = .003, 
respectively). The rate of grade 3–4 AEs was higher in the mFOLFIRINOX 
group (75.9% vs. 52.9%).
Chemoradiation 
The Panel recommendations for chemoradiation in ampullary 
adenocarcinoma are similar to those in the Oncology Guidelines for 
Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma, Oncology Guidelines for Biliary Tract Cancers, 
and Oncology Guidelines for Small Bowel Adenocarcinoma (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines). Capecitabine + concurrent RT and 5-FU + concurrent 
RT are options for pancreatobiliary, mixed, and intestinal types, while 
gemcitabine + concurrent RT is recommended for pancreatobiliary type 
only. All three regimens have been investigated in the adjuvant setting in 
small, retrospective, and single-institutional studies.26,86,104,114,115,118,126 5-
FU–based chemoradiation regimens were commonly used in these 
studies. An analysis of data from NCDB in which patients received 
adjuvant chemotherapy (n = 870) or adjuvant chemoradiation (n = 669) 
revealed that adjuvant chemotherapy use increased (9%–32% between 
2004–2005 and 2012–2013) during the same time that adjuvant 
chemoradiation use decreased (20%–12%).115 Contradictory studies exist
with respect to the usefulness of adjuvant chemoradiation in patients with 
ampullary cancer.86,104,114,115,126 A systematic review and meta-analysis of 
10 retrospective studies, including 3361 patients with ampullary cancer, 
looked at adjuvant RT delivered with concurrent chemotherapy (mostly 5-
FU). Adjuvant chemoradiation significantly reduced the risk of death (HR, 
0.75; P = .01).118 Several studies show that adjuvant chemoradiation 
particularly benefits patients who have node-positive disease.114,118,119 A 
phase III, randomized EORTC trial tested adjuvant chemoradiation with 5-
FU versus observation after surgery in patients with pancreatic head and 
periampullary cancers. Of the 103 patients assigned to the observation 
arm, 57 had pancreatic cancer, 44 had periampullary cancer, and 2 were 
unknown; of the 104 patients assigned to the treatment arm, 55 had 
pancreatic cancer, 48 had periampullary cancer, and 1 was unknown. The 
number of patients with ampullary cancer was not reported in this study. 
The final analysis showed no significant difference in median or 2-year 
survival rates between the two arms.138 There is more high-level evidence 
on chemoradiation in the setting of pancreatic cancer, such as data from 
ESPAC-1, which can be extrapolated to ampullary cancer.139 For more 
information on these studies, see the Discussion section of the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma (available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
Treatment of Metastatic Disease 
Patients diagnosed with metastatic ampullary adenocarcinomas should 
obtain genetic testing for hereditary mutations and molecular profiling of 
tumor tissue, if not previously done. Chemoradiation may be used for 
palliative indications. For select patients with oligometastatic disease and 
response/stable disease to systemic therapy, local-directed therapy to liver 
or lung metastases may be considered after multidisciplinary tumor board 
review. Patients with poor performance status (PS) should also receive 
palliative and best supportive care or palliative RT.

Ampullary Adenocarcinoma
MS-13
First-Line Systemic Therapy 
For pancreatobiliary/mixed type ampullary cancer with good and 
intermediate PS, the Panel included albumin-bound 
paclitaxel/gemcitabine, capecitabine/gemcitabine, cisplatin/gemcitabine, 
cisplatin/durvalumab/gemcitabine, and FOLFOX. For good PS 0–1 only 
the Panel also recommends FOLFIRINOX/mFOLFIRINOX, and 
NALIRIFOX (5-FU/liposomal irinotecan/leucovorin/oxaliplatin). The 
recommendations for these regimens are derived from the Oncology Guidelines 
Guidelines for Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma and the Oncology Guidelines for 
Biliary Tract Cancers (available at www.Oncology Guidelines), with the Panel adding 
FOLFOX based on their clinical experience. These recommendations are 
mostly based on phase II/III randomized data for albumin-bound 
paclitaxel/gemcitabine,140 capecitabine/gemcitabine,141 
cisplatin/gemcitabine,129 cisplatin/durvalumab/gemcitabine,142 and 
FOLFIRINOX/mFOLFIRINOX.143,144 For more information on these 
studies, see the Discussion section of the Oncology Guidelines for Pancreatic 
Adenocarcinoma and Oncology Guidelines for Biliary Tract Cancers 
(available at www.Oncology Guidelines). For pancreatobiliary/mixed type ampullary 
cancer with poor PS, simplified formulations of certain regimens can be 
utilized; however, providing palliative and best supportive care are 
preferred.
For intestinal type ampullary cancer, all recommendations are derived 
from the Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines). Most of the recommendations are based on phase II/III, 
randomized data for CapeOx ± bevacizumab,145 FOLFIRI (5-
FU//irinotecan/leucovorin) ± bevacizumab,146 FOLFIRINOX ± 
bevacizumab,147 and FOLFOX ± bevacizumab.145,148 While all of these 
options are appropriate for patients with good and intermediate PS, 5-FU 
+ leucovorin and capecitabine are considered appropriate options for 
those with intermediate PS, with the same rationale of reducing toxicity.148-
150 For poor PS, the systemic therapy options include capecitabine and 5-
FU/leucovorin. For more information on these studies, see the Discussion 
section of the Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines). Due to the controversial role and scant available data for 
anti-EGFR therapies in KRAS wild-type small bowel cancers or for right-
sided colon cancers, the Panel does not recommend using anti-EGFR 
therapies for KRAS wild-type ampullary adenocarcinomas of intestinal 
subtype.
In addition to chemotherapy recommendations, pembrolizumab is a 
recommended option for all ampullary adenocarcinomas with MSI-H, 
dMMR, or TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb), and ipilimumab + nivolumab is a 
recommended option for MSI-H, dMMR intestinal type ampullary 
adenocarcinomas. However, due to higher toxicity of the nivolumab plus 
ipilumumab combination, nivolumab monotherapy is recommended for 
patients with poor PS. Patients can also receive entrectinib or larotrectinib 
or repotrectinib if the tumors test positive for NTRK gene fusion. 
Selpercatinib is also recommended for tumors with RET fusion positivity. 
These options are applicable regardless of PS. With the exception of 
ipilimumab + nivolumab, the rationale for which comes from the metastatic 
colorectal cancer setting, the other regimens are supported by basket 
trials that include many different cancer types. No ampullary cancer or a 
very small number of periampullary cancers were included in these 
studies. Therefore, cautious extrapolation of data from these studies to the 
ampullary cancer setting is prudent.
The recommendation for pembrolizumab is supported by data from the 
phase II KEYNOTE-518 study, in which a total of 233 patients 
representing 27 tumor types were treated with pembrolizumab after 
disease progression with prior therapy.151 The objective response rate was 
34.3%, median PFS was 4.1 months, and median OS was 23.5 months. 
The rate of grade 3–5 treatment-related AEs was 14.6%. No patient with 
ampullary cancer was enrolled in this trial.

MS-14
The recommendation for ipilimumab + nivolumab is based on data in the 
metastatic colorectal cancer setting, hence its suitability only for intestinal 
type ampullary cancer.152 In the phase II CheckMate 142 study, patients 
with no prior treatment received ipilimumab + nivolumab until disease 
progression. The objective response rate was 69%, while 2-year PFS and 
2-year OS were 74% and 79%, respectively. The rate of grade 3–4 
treatment-related AEs was 22%. This trial also included a cohort of 
previously treated patients. In this group (n = 119), the objective response 
rate was 55% and the 1-year PFS and 1-year OS were 71% and 85%, 
respectively. The rate of grade 3–4 treatment-related AEs was 32%.153
The recommendations for larotrectinib and entrectinib are supported by 
data from two phase I–II basket trials.154,155 The first enrolled 55 patients, 
including 4 with colon cancer, 2 with cholangiocarcinoma, and 1 with 
pancreatic cancer, who were treated with larotrectinib. The overall 
response rate was 75%, and neither the median duration of response nor 
the median PFS was reached after a median follow-up of 9.9 months.154 
The second basket trial enrolled 54 patients, including 4 with colorectal 
cancer, 3 with pancreatic cancer, and 1 with cholangiocarcinoma. 
Objective response was noted in 57% of patients and the median PFS and 
median OS were 11 months and 21 months, respectively.155 Patients in 
both of these trials had received prior systemic therapy. The FDA issued 
accelerated approval for repotrectinib, another NTRK-based regimen, for 
adult and pediatric patients ≥12 years with solid tumors that have NTRK 
gene fusions, are locally advanced or metastatic or where surgical 
resection is likely to result in severe morbidity, and that have progressed 
following standard treatment or have no satisfactory alternative therapy. A 
small number of patients with pancreatic adenocarcinoma were evaluated 
in the TRIDENT trial; however no ampullary cancers were included.156
Selpercatinib, a kinase inhibitor, received accelerated approval from the 
FDA based on treatment of RET fusion-positive non-small cell lung
cancer, medullary thyroid, and thyroid cancers. The recommendations are 
based on the trial that included 11 patients with pancreatic 
adenocarcinoma, at least 50% of whom had objective response rates.157
Based on the FDA approvals, dabrafenib + trametinib can be used as a 
treatment option for BRAF V600E-mutated ampullary adenocarcinomas, 
regardless of PS. NCI-MATCH Subprotocol H, an open-label study that 
evaluated dabrafenib + trametinib in patients with solid tumors, 
lymphomas, or multiple myeloma whose tumors harbored a BRAF V600E 
mutation, the response rate was 37.9% (n = 29), with a median duration of 
response of 25.1 months. With a median follow-up of 23 months, median 
OS was 28.6 months and median PFS was 11.4 months.158 ROAR, a 
phase II, open-label basket trial in patients with BRAF V600E-mutated rare 
cancers showed that in 43 patients with BRAF V600E-mutated biliary tract 
cancer, the response rate was 47% (by independent reviewer). Median 
OS was 14 months and median PFS was 9 months.159
Treatment for Disease Progression 
Patients with disease progression and good/intermediate PS should 
preferably be enrolled in clinical trials. Alternative options are systemic 
chemotherapy, or possibly targeted therapy based on molecular profiling 
as clinically indicated, or palliative RT for severe pain refractory to 
analgesic therapy. Patients with disease progression and poor PS should 
receive palliative and best supportive care and be considered for systemic 
therapy, targeted therapy based on molecular profiling as clinically 
indicated, or palliative RT. For specific systemic therapy regimen 
recommendations, see Principles of Systemic Therapy in the algorithm. 
For anyone receiving therapy for disease progression, serial imaging is 
recommended as indicated to assess disease response.
Systemic Therapy Options 
In case of disease progression, the rule of thumb is that any regimen other 
than the one used in the first-line setting is a possible option. In patients

with good/intermediate PS and pancreatobiliary/mixed type ampullary 
cancer previously treated with a gemcitabine-based regimen, a 
fluoropyrimidine-based regimen is recommended for subsequent-line 
therapy. Capecitabine,160 CapeOx,161 5-FU/leucovorin,127,162,163 5-
FU/leucovorin/liposomal irinotecan,162,164,165 FOLFIRI,166-169 
FOLFIRINOX170 or mFOLFIRINOX and FOLFOX,171-175 and OFF (5-
FU/leucovorin/oxaliplatin)163,176 are recommended options. Conversely, in 
patients with good/intermediate PS and pancreatobiliary/mixed type 
ampullary cancer previously treated with a fluoropyrimidine-based 
regimen, a gemcitabine-based regimen is recommended for subsequent-
line therapy. Albumin-bound paclitaxel/gemcitabine, 
capecitabine/gemcitabine, and gemcitabine can be used. In addition, 
FOLFIRI166,167 or 5-FU/leucovorin/liposomal irinotecan162 can be tried in 
case of no progression on prior irinotecan or oxaliplatin. In patients with 
poor PS and pancreatobiliary/mixed type ampullary cancer, 
gemcitabine,160 capecitabine,160 or 5-FU/leucovorin162,163 can be used. 
Some of these regimens have been added based on the Panel’s clinical 
expertise.
In addition to these agents, cisplatin/gemcitabine for known 
BRCA1/2/PALB2 mutations is recommended for patients with 
good/intermediate PS. This recommendation is supported by data from a 
phase II randomized trial where 50 patients with previously untreated 
pancreatic cancer with germline BRCA/PALB2 mutations were randomly 
assigned to cisplatin/gemcitabine or cisplatin/gemcitabine/veliparib. The 
response rate was 74.1% and 65.2% for each arm, respectively; median 
PFS was 10.1 months and 9.7 months, and median OS was 15.5 months 
and 16.4 months, respectively.177
Similar to recommendations for the first-line setting, all of the 
recommendations for the subsequent-line setting are derived from phase 
II/III data in pancreatic cancer or biliary cancer, with the exception of the
reference for FOLFIRINOX, which is an exploratory analysis of the 
MPACT trial.170 For more information on these studies, see the Discussion 
sections of the Oncology Guidelines for Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma and 
Oncology Guidelines for Biliary Tract Cancers (available at www.Oncology Guidelines).
For patients with good PS and intestinal type ampullary cancer, FOLFIRI ± 
bevacizumab is a possible subsequent-line option for patients previously 
treated with an oxaliplatin-based regimen. Capecitabine ± bevacizumab, 
CapeOx ± bevacizumab,145 5-FU/leucovorin ± bevacizumab, or FOLFOX ± 
bevacizumab145,148 can be used for patients with intermediate PS and 
previously treated with an irinotecan-based regimen. For patients with 
poor PS and intestinal type ampullary cancer, palliative/best supportive 
care are preferred; however, capecitabine or 5-FU/Leucovorin are also 
options. Similar to recommendations for the first-line setting, all of the 
recommendations for the subsequent-line setting are derived from phase 
II/III data in colon cancer. For more information on these studies, see the 
Discussion section of the Oncology Guidelines for Colon Cancer (available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines).
Targeted therapy regimens recommended in the first-line setting are also 
possible options for disease progression. They are similar to 
recommendations for metastatic disease (see algorithm for specific 
regimens). Trials demonstrating the efficacy of these regimens included all 
or a portion of patients who had received and progressed on prior 
systemic therapy.151,153-155,157-159
Furthermore, dostarlimab-gxly is recommended for patients with any PS in 
tumors with MSI-H or dMMR. This recommendation is based on results of 
a phase I basket study where a total of 209 patients, including 99 with 
gastrointestinal cancer (69 with colorectal cancer), received dostarlimab-
gxly until disease progression or discontinuation. The objective response 
rate was 41.6%.178

MS-16
Additionally, HER2 overexpression occurs in 13% of ampullary cancers, 
and HER2 targeting is relevant. In 2024, the FDA granted accelerated 
approval to fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki for adult patients with 
unresectable or metastatic HER2-positive (IHC3+) solid tumors who 
have received prior systemic treatment and have no satisfactory 
alternative treatment options. The DESTINY-PanTumor2 trial included a 
total of 25 patients (all with good PS) with advanced or metastatic 
pancreatic cancer, with <20% of patients having tumors that expressed 
IHC 3+ HER2.179 In this trial, 16 patients with BTCs with HER IHC3+ 
expression, the investigator-assessed ORR, median OS, and median PFS 
were 56.3%, 12.4 months, and 7.4 months, respectively. The median OS 
and PFS were 7.1 months (95% CI, 4.7%–14.6%), 5.1 months (95% CI, 
3.0%–7.3%), respectively in 22 evaluable patients with HER2-positive 
advanced BTCs in the HERB trial.180 In patients with advanced colon 
cancer and HER2 expression (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+/ISH+) the primary 
endpoint of ORR was 45.3%, with one complete response and 23 partial 
responses.181 Given the similarity between ampullary adenocarcinoma 
and biliary tract tumors along with intestinal subtypes addressed in these 
guidelines, the Panel recommends fam-trastuzumab as a subsequent 
therapy option only for patients with good PS and HER2 IHC 3+ 
expression.
Palliative and Best Supportive Care
The recommendations for palliative and best supportive care are the same 
as those for pancreatic adenocarcinoma (see Discussion section in the 
Oncology Guidelines for Pancreatic Adenocarcinoma, available at 
www.Oncology Guidelines).
Summary
In summary, these guidelines discuss primary treatment for ampullary 
adenomas and adenocarcinomas, which consist of appropriate surgical, 
RT, and systemic therapy options including neoadjuvant, adjuvant, and 
subsequent lines of therapy.
